timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — "Search Through Her Streets for One Honest Person"

heb.paragraph: God commands a search for anyone who "deals honestly" (ʿōśeh mišpāṭ) and "seeks the truth" (mĕbaqqēš ʾĕmûnâ). The pairing of mišpāṭ and ʾĕmûnâ — justice and faithfulness — represents the two pillars of covenant society. One person practicing both would save the city. The echo of Abraham bargaining for Sodom (Gen 18:22–33) is unmistakable, but here the number has dropped from ten to one — and even one cannot be found.

refs.note: Abraham's intercession for Sodom established the principle that righteous individuals could avert judgment on a city. Jeremiah's search reduces the number to one — and fails.
Paul quotes Psalm 14 to make the same universal claim: "There is no one righteous, not even one." Jeremiah 5 provides the prophetic evidence for Paul's theological argument.
Ezekiel echoes Jeremiah: "I looked for someone to stand in the gap... but I found no one." The search theme recurs as a prophetic motif.

notes.note: The search for one righteous person is commissioned by God himself. The command "roam through the streets" suggests an exhaustive, systematic investigation — not a casual glance but a thorough audit of the city's moral condition.
"You struck them, but they felt no pain; you crushed them, but they refused correction." Past judgments have not produced repentance — the people have developed spiritual calluses.
Jeremiah first excuses the poor ("they are foolish, they do not know") but then turns to the leaders and finds them equally corrupt. Ignorance explains the masses; the leaders have no excuse.

God's rhetorical question: "Why should I forgive you?" The grounds for pardon have been systematically eliminated. The question is not vindictive but genuinely forensic — what basis remains for acquittal?

Calvin draws the connection to Sodom: "If God would have spared Sodom for ten righteous, how much more Jerusalem for one? But the one cannot be found. The moral state of the covenant people is now worse than that of the quintessential wicked city."
Calvin observes that insensitivity to divine correction is the final stage of spiritual decline. When God's chastening no longer produces pain, the conscience has been cauterized.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The command to "go up and down the streets" uses the verb šôṭĕṭû, which suggests systematic searching in every quarter of the city — public squares, marketplaces, residential areas.
The phrase "they committed adultery" may refer to both literal sexual immorality and metaphorical spiritual unfaithfulness — the prophetic double entendre collapses the distinction.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies this as a "search narrative" that deliberately echoes the Sodom tradition. The descending threshold (from ten to one) heightens the pathos: Jerusalem has fallen below even the minimal standard that would have saved the most infamous city in Israelite memory.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the failed search as a critique of the entire social order. Neither the poor (who lack knowledge) nor the rich (who have rejected knowledge) practice justice. The problem is structural, not individual — the system produces injustice at every level.

hist.context: The search for one righteous person (v.1) recalls Abraham's intercession for Sodom in Genesis 18, but with a devastating difference: there God would spare the city for ten righteous people; here the threshold is one, and even that fails. Jeremiah examines both the poor ("they are foolish," v.4) and the leaders ("they too had broken the yoke," v.5), finding universal corruption across every social class.

sections.header: Verses 10–19 — "They Have Lied About the LORD"

heb.paragraph: The people have "denied" (kiḥăšû) the LORD (v.12), saying "He will do nothing! No harm will come to us." The verb kāḥaš means to deny, disown, or deceive — Israel has functionally denied God's existence as an active agent in history. This is not philosophical atheism but practical atheism — living as though God does not act.

refs.note: The false prophets who say "No harm will come to you" are more fully denounced in chapter 23 — they speak visions from their own minds, not from God's mouth.
Peter warns of scoffers who say "Where is this coming he promised?" — the same denial of divine action that Jeremiah confronts.

notes.note: The people deny God's activity in history: "He will do nothing!" The prophets are dismissed as "wind." This is practical atheism dressed in religious language — maintaining cultic observance while denying God's sovereign agency.
God turns the accusation back: since the prophets are "wind," God will make his word fire and the people fuel. The metaphor reverses the people's dismissal — what they called wind becomes an inferno.

The invading nation is described in detail: ancient, enduring, speaking an unknown language. They will devour harvest, flocks, vines, and fortified cities — comprehensive destruction of both agriculture and infrastructure.

Calvin identifies this as the perennial human error: mistaking God's patience for God's absence. Because judgment is delayed, they conclude it will never come. Calvin calls this "the security of those who sleep in their sins."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew haddōbēr ("the one who speaks") is used for the false prophets. The contrast is between prophets as "wind" (rûaḥ) and God's word as "fire" (ʾēš) — insubstantial speech versus consuming power.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom notes the ironic reversal: the people say the prophets are "wind" (rûaḥ, v.13), but God converts this dismissal into judgment language — "I am making my words in your mouth fire" (v.14). Their own contempt becomes the instrument of their destruction.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies the people's denial as a form of "managed reality" — controlling the narrative to exclude the possibility of divine judgment. The false prophets function as propagandists for the status quo, reassuring the powerful that their power is secure.

hist.context: The false prophets are singled out for special condemnation: they are "wind" (rûaḥ) rather than divine word (v.13). The wordplay is sharp — rûaḥ can mean both "wind" and "spirit," but here it means empty breath, mere hot air. Because the prophets are wind, God will make his word in Jeremiah's mouth into fire, and the people into wood that the fire consumes.

heb.paragraph: The adjective sākāl ("senseless," v.21) describes a people who "have eyes but do not see, who have ears but do not hear." This is not cognitive failure but perceptual dysfunction — the senses work but meaning does not register. The idiom of having organs of perception that fail to perceive recurs throughout prophetic literature (Isa 6:9–10, Ezek 12:2) and becomes a key theme in Jesus' parables (Mark 4:12).

refs.note: Isaiah's commission to dull the people's perception uses the same language of eyes that don't see and ears that don't hear.
Jesus echoes Jeremiah directly: "Do you have eyes but fail to see, and ears but fail to hear?" — prophetic frustration with spiritual obtuseness.
Amos similarly pairs religious failure with social injustice: worship without justice is an abomination.

notes.note: God set the sand as the boundary for the sea — even the ocean obeys its limits, but Israel transgresses all divine boundaries. Nature is more obedient than the covenant people.
The rich are indicted for specific crimes: setting traps for people, growing fat from exploitation, failing to defend the fatherless. Apostasy always produces social injustice.

"A horrible and shocking thing" — prophets lie, priests self-deal, and the people love it. The system is self-reinforcing: corrupt leaders and complicit followers sustain each other in mutual deception.

Calvin draws the distinction between natural and spiritual perception: the organs function but the soul does not engage. This is what theologians call "total depravity" — not that every faculty is destroyed, but that every faculty is misdirected.

Calvin calls this verse "the most terrifying in Jeremiah" — not because of God's judgment but because of the people's willing complicity. When the masses prefer comfortable lies to hard truth, the prophets of deception will never lack an audience.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The rhetorical question about the sea boundary draws on creation theology — God's ordering of chaos (Gen 1:9–10). Israel's rebellion is portrayed as a return to pre-creation disorder, connecting to the tōhû wābōhû vision of 4:23.

The Hebrew "they have grown fat and sleek" (šāmĕnû ʿāšĕtû) uses vocabulary associated with well-fed livestock — the wealthy have become like pampered animals, insensible to human suffering around them.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies vv.20–31 as a "summary indictment" that gathers the major themes of chapters 2–5: spiritual blindness (v.21), violation of natural order (vv.22–24), social exploitation (vv.26–28), and institutional corruption (vv.30–31). The section functions as the peroration of the first major speech cycle.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies a "triad of dysfunction" — prophets, priests, and people form an interlocking system of deception. The final question "What will you do in the end?" breaks the fourth wall, addressing the reader directly and demanding a response that the text itself cannot provide.

hist.context: The final section broadens the indictment to include social injustice alongside spiritual apostasy. The rich "grow fat and sleek" (v.28) while failing to defend the orphan and the needy. The chapter closes with a horrifying statement: "the prophets prophesy lies, the priests rule by their own authority, and my people love it this way" (v.31). The people are not merely victims of corrupt leadership — they are willing participants in their own deception.

themes.note: Judgment and Justice both at 9 — the chapter pairs divine judgment with social injustice. The failed search for one righteous person drives both themes. Mercy at 2 reflects the near-total absence of hope in this chapter.

rows.label: vv.1–6
rows.text: The Search: Not One Righteous Found

rows.label: vv.20–29
rows.text: Eyes That Cannot See, Ears That Cannot Hear

lit.note: The chapter builds from a failed search for righteousness (vv.1–6) through denial of divine action (vv.10–17) to a comprehensive indictment of spiritual blindness and social injustice (vv.20–31). The closing verse — "my people love it this way" — is the devastating punchline.

hebtext.note: God commands a search for anyone who "deals honestly" (ʿōśeh mišpāṭ) and "seeks the truth" (mĕbaqqēš ʾĕmûnâ). The pairing of mišpāṭ and ʾĕmûnâ — justice and faithfulness — represents the two pillars of covenant society. One person practicing both would save the city. The echo of Abraham bargaining for Sodom (Gen 18:22–33) is unmistakable, but here the number has dropped from ten to one — and even one cannot be found.

The people have "denied" (kiḥăšû) the LORD (v.12), saying "He will do nothing! No harm will come to us." The verb kāḥaš means to deny, disown, or deceive — Israel has functionally denied God's existence as an active agent in history. This is not philosophical atheism but practical atheism — living as though God does not act.

The adjective sākāl ("senseless," v.21) describes a people who "have eyes but do not see, who have ears but do not hear." This is not cognitive failure but perceptual dysfunction — the senses work but meaning does not register. The idiom of having organs of perception that fail to perceive recurs throughout prophetic literature (Isa 6:9–10, Ezek 12:2) and becomes a key theme in Jesus' parables (Mark 4:12).

trans.title: Comparing translations in Not One Righteous

translations.text: Lord, do not your eyes look for truth? You struck them, but they felt no pain; you crushed them, but they refused correction. They made their faces harder than stone and refused to repent.

O LORD, do not your eyes look for truth? You have struck them down, but they felt no anguish; you have consumed them, but they refused to take correction. They have made their faces harder than rock; they have refused to repent.

Therefore a lion from the forest will attack them, a wolf from the desert will ravage them, a leopard will lie in wait near their towns to tear to pieces any who venture out, for their rebellion is great and their backslidings many.

Therefore a lion from the forest shall strike them down; a wolf from the desert shall devastate them. A leopard is watching their cities; everyone who goes out of them shall be torn in pieces, because their transgressions are many, their apostasies are great.

debate: The rhetorical question about the sea boundary draws on creation theology — God's ordering of chaos (Gen 1:9–10). Israel's rebellion is portrayed as a…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Not One Righteous within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Abraham's intercession for Sodom established the principle that righteous individuals could avert judgment on a city. Jeremiah's search reduces the number to one — and fails.
Paul quotes Psalm 14 to make the same universal claim: "There is no one righteous, not even one." Jeremiah 5 provides the prophetic evidence for Paul's theological argument.
Ezekiel echoes Jeremiah: "I looked for someone to stand in the gap... but I found no one." The search theme recurs as a prophetic motif.
The false prophets who say "No harm will come to you" are more fully denounced in chapter 23 — they speak visions from their own minds, not from God's mouth.
Peter warns of scoffers who say "Where is this coming he promised?" — the same denial of divine action that Jeremiah confronts.
Isaiah's commission to dull the people's perception uses the same language of eyes that don't see and ears that don't hear.

coaching.tip: 'Go up and down the streets of Jerusalem, look around and consider, search through her squares. If you can find but one person who deals honestly — I will forgive this city' (v. 1). The echo of Abraham bargaining for Sodom (Genesis 18:32) is unmistakable — but there, Abraham negotiated down to ten. Jeremiah's God lowers the bar to one, and the city still fails. Jerusalem is worse than Sodom.

'A horrible and shocking thing has happened in the land: the prophets prophesy lies, the priests rule by their own authority, and my people love it this way' (vv. 30–31). The last clause is the most damning. The people aren't victims of corrupt leadership — they prefer it. Jeremiah diagnoses demand-side corruption: false prophecy thrives because there's a market for comfortable lies.
